structured_choices.data_source: Anonymized session logs from a large e-commerce platform spanning 18 months of production data. Each row represents a complete session (from first page view to purchase or session exit within 30 days). Data includes timestamped clickstream events (page views, searches, clicks, cart additions/removals), per-event dwell times in seconds, device metadata (type, OS, browser), traffic source/referral channel, and binary label: whether the session resulted in a purchase within 30 calendar days of session start.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: conversion = 1 if any purchase transaction is attributed to the session within 30 days of first page view; 0 otherwise. Attribution is first-touch within the 30-day window. Label is determined at the end of the 30-day observation window, so no leakage of future purchase information into training.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by traffic source. Chronologically divide the 18-month dataset into three periods: (1) months 1–12 (training), (2) months 13–15 (validation), (3) months 16–18 (test). Within each period, stratify by top 10 acquisition channels (organic, paid_search, paid_social, referral, direct, etc.) to ensure each channel is represented proportionally in train/val/test. This temporal split prevents information leakage (a session's future events cannot inform its training) and respects the time-series nature of marketplace dynamics, campaign seasonality, and model deployment drift. Stratification ensures the baseline and sequential model are each evaluated fairly across channel segments, preventing spurious gains that exploit channel-specific imbalances.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are fit on the training set only. Validation and test sets are transformed using the training-fitted transformers. This includes (a) StandardScaler or RobustScaler fitted on train dwell-time distributions, (b) OneHotEncoder or target-encoding for device type and traffic source fitted on train data, (c) any missing-value imputation strategies (e.g., median dwell time per device type) fit on train. Event-sequence preprocessing (e.g., padding, truncation) is also parameterized from train data.
structured_choices.features: Baseline (static) features: (1) user-level aggregates (if user ID available, omit to avoid first-time buyer tracking issues; use session-only aggregates instead), (2) session-level aggregates: total dwell time (sum), mean dwell time per event, event count (proxy for engagement), (3) device features: device_type (mobile/desktop/tablet), OS, browser, (4) traffic source: acquisition_channel (one-hot or target-encoded), referrer domain, (5) temporal: hour_of_day, day_of_week, is_weekend. Sequential model features: (1) all baseline features, (2) ordered sequence of clickstream events (page type, search term, product category, cart action type), (3) per-event dwell times (in seconds, clipped at 99th percentile to avoid outliers), (4) event-level features (e.g., product price range if viewed, product rating if available). No feature should use information from events occurring after the prediction point (e.g., checkout page visits must not leak into training if predicting at an earlier session stage).
structured_choices.model: Sequential model: a unidirectional LSTM or Transformer encoder (e.g., a 2-layer Transformer with 8 attention heads) that ingests the ordered sequence of (event_type, dwell_time, product_features) embeddings and outputs a session-level representation, which is concatenated with baseline static features and fed to a shallow dense classifier (1–2 layers, 64–128 units) with sigmoid output. Dropout (0.3–0.5) is applied to regularize overfitting on imbalanced data. The model is trained end-to-end with class-weighted cross-entropy loss (weight inverse to class frequency) to account for class imbalance.
structured_choices.baseline: Static logistic regression model trained on the same baseline feature set (device, traffic source, temporal, session-level aggregates like event count and total dwell time). No access to event sequence or per-event dwell times. Logistic regression is regularized with L2 penalty (tuned via validation set AUC). Both the baseline and sequential model receive equal tuning effort: hyperparameter grids are searched via grid or random search over 50–100 configurations per model, using validation set AUC. Both models are trained with the same class-weighting scheme. This ensures the comparison isolates the predictive value of sequence information, not differences in model capacity or tuning depth.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: (1) AUROC is threshold-invariant and reflects the model's ability to rank-order sessions by conversion likelihood — directly aligned with the business goal of identifying high-intent visitors for targeted interventions. (2) AUROC is robust to class imbalance (unlike accuracy or F1 at a fixed threshold), so it fairly compares both models on the heavily imbalanced test set. (3) AUROC enables downstream users (marketing, product) to select an operating point (threshold) that balances precision and recall based on intervention budget, without the model being over-fit to a particular threshold.
secondary_metrics: Average Precision (PR-AUC): area under the Precision-Recall curve, more sensitive to minority class performance than AUROC and useful for confirming gains hold at high precision regimes where interventions are most cost-effective.

Calibration (Expected Calibration Error, ECE): measure of whether predicted probabilities match observed frequencies, important because marketing teams will use predicted probabilities to prioritize budgets. Poorly calibrated models can waste spend.
Performance by acquisition channel (AUROC per top 5 channels): ensures the sequential model generalizes across different user intent profiles and is not exploiting channel-specific artifacts.
Session length stratification (AUROC for short, medium, long sessions): controls for the confound that the sequential model might simply be capturing session length rather than intent signals.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 13–15). A grid of 50–100 hyperparameter combinations per model (learning rate, hidden dimension, dropout, L2 penalty, batch size, etc.) is evaluated by training on the training set and measuring AUROC on the validation set. The single best configuration (highest validation AUROC) is selected. The test set (months 16–18) is held out and used exactly once, at the end, to compute final performance metrics. No data snooping or threshold adjustment on test data is permitted.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is locked and evaluated exactly once at the end. Both the baseline and sequential model are retrained on the combined training + validation data (months 1–15) using the hyperparameters selected on the validation set, then evaluated once on the held-out test set (months 16–18). This maximizes training signal while respecting the temporal split and avoiding test-set contamination.
structured_choices.confound_controls: (1) Static baseline as a control: the logistic regression baseline uses only aggregated features (device, traffic source, dwell-time totals), so any improvement by the sequential model cannot be attributed to these features alone. (2) Session-length stratification on test set: compute AUROC separately for sessions in the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles of event count to confirm the sequential model is not simply learning 'longer sessions convert more' — a channel or campaign artifact. (3) Acquisition channel stratification: compute AUROC per top 5 channels on the test set; report the average and range. If one channel shows huge improvement and others show none, this signals channel-specific overfitting rather than genuine intent modeling. (4) Temporal split: prevents leakage of future events into training and ensures the model is evaluated in a realistic deployment scenario where it must predict on data from a later time period than training. (5) Class-weighted loss in both models: ensures both are equally incentivized to handle imbalance, isolating the benefit of sequence modeling.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Sequential Clickstream Model for First-Time Buyer Conversion Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

This experiment uses anonymized session logs spanning 18 months from the production e-commerce platform. Each record represents a complete session: a visitor's journey from first page view to session exit or purchase. For each session, we retain the full ordered sequence of clickstream events (page type, search queries, product views, cart actions), per-event dwell times in seconds, device metadata (type, OS, browser), traffic source (acquisition channel), and a binary label indicating whether the visitor made a purchase within 30 calendar days of session start. The label is finalized after the 30-day attribution window closes, ensuring no future information leaks into training.

The data is known to be imbalanced: typically 2–5% of first-time sessions convert to purchase. We accept this and design all components (loss functions, metrics, splits) to handle imbalance explicitly rather than downsample or oversample, which would distort the temporal and channel distributions.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split and Temporal Stratification

We divide the 18 months chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–18 for the held-out test set. This temporal split is essential: it prevents the model from seeing events that occur after the prediction point (e.g., a page view from day 5 of a session cannot train on a purchase from day 25) and simulates realistic deployment, where the model must predict on future data unseen during development.

Within each time period, we stratify by the top 10 acquisition channels (organic search, paid search, paid social, email, referral, direct, affiliate, etc.) to ensure channels are represented proportionally across all three sets. This stratification serves two purposes: it prevents the sequential model from exploiting channel-specific imbalances (e.g., paid_social visitors may convert at different baseline rates), and it allows us to measure model performance within key acquisition channels on the test set, a critical check for channel-specific overfitting.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

We construct two feature sets: **baseline (static) features** and **sequential features**.

**Baseline features** (used by both models): device type (mobile/desktop/tablet, one-hot encoded), operating system, browser, acquisition channel (one-hot or target-encoded), referrer domain, temporal features (hour of day, day of week, is_weekend), and session-level aggregates derived from the event sequence: total dwell time, mean dwell time per event, event count, and product category distribution (e.g., % of events on electronics, apparel, etc.).

**Sequential features** (for the sequential model only): the ordered sequence of event types (page view, search, product view, cart add, cart remove, checkout), product-level features if available (price bucket, rating), and per-event dwell times (clipped at the 99th percentile to prevent outliers from dominating embeddings).

All scaling and encoding transformers (StandardScaler for dwell times, OneHotEncoder for device type, target-encoding for traffic source) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. This ensures we do not learn statistics from future data.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline Comparison

**Sequential model**: A 2-layer Transformer encoder with 8 attention heads ingests the ordered event sequence (each event is embedded as [event_type_embedding, dwell_time_normalized]) and produces a session-level representation via mean-pooling across the sequence. This representation is concatenated with baseline static features, passed through a 2-layer dense network (128 units each, ReLU, 0.4 dropout), and output via sigmoid for binary probability. The model is trained with class-weighted cross-entropy loss (positive class weight = total negatives / total positives) to account for imbalance.

**Baseline model**: Logistic regression trained on the baseline feature set only (no access to event sequence). Regularized with L2 penalty (C tuned on validation set). Both models receive equal hyperparameter tuning effort: we grid-search 50–100 configurations per model (learning rate, embedding dimension, dropout, L2 penalty, batch size) using the validation set AUROC as the selection criterion. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of sequence information, not differences in tuning depth or model capacity.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve).** AUROC is chosen because (1) it is threshold-invariant, reflecting the model's ranking ability, which aligns directly with the business goal of identifying high-intent visitors so marketing can allocate limited incentives efficiently, (2) it is robust to class imbalance, providing a fair comparison despite the 2–5% positive rate, and (3) it allows downstream users to select an operating point (threshold) based on their intervention budget without the model being overfitted to a single threshold.

**Secondary metrics**: (a) Average Precision (area under the Precision-Recall curve), which is more sensitive to minority class performance and relevant for high-precision intervention scenarios; (b) Expected Calibration Error (ECE), measuring whether predicted probabilities match observed frequencies, critical because marketing teams will use probabilities to prioritize spend; (c) per-channel AUROC stratification to detect channel-specific overfitting; (d) session-length stratified AUROC to control for the confound that the sequential model might simply learn 'longer sessions convert more.'

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 13–15). For each model, we train on the training set with different hyperparameter configurations, evaluate each on the validation set AUROC, and select the single best configuration. The test set (months 16–18) is locked and untouched during this phase. At the end, both the baseline and sequential models are retrained on the combined training + validation data (months 1–15) using the selected hyperparameters, then evaluated exactly once on the test set. This maximizes training signal while respecting the temporal boundary.

### 7. Confound Control and Claim Scope

We control for confounds as follows: (1) the baseline model uses only static aggregates, so any improvement by the sequential model cannot be attributed to device, traffic source, or simple engagement metrics; (2) per-channel AUROC stratification on the test set confirms the sequential model generalizes across acquisition channels and is not exploiting channel-specific patterns; (3) session-length stratification (25th, 50th, 75th percentile of event count) confirms the model is learning intent signals, not just 'longer sessions convert;' (4) temporal split prevents future information leakage and simulates realistic deployment; (5) class-weighted loss in both models ensures fair comparison on imbalanced data.

This design establishes whether a sequential model trained on clickstream events and dwell times predicts 30-day purchase conversion better than a static baseline. It does **not** establish causation (the model correlates with intent, it does not cause it), nor does it quantify the offline impact of deploying this model on marketing interventions — that would require a randomized intervention experiment downstream.
